cataloging_subject.label: The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The manuscript-specific textual and editorial unit represented by Frederick J. Furnivall's Chaucer Society one-text edition and the U-M item agz8234: Chaucer's unfinished Canterbury Tales in Furnivall's Gg/Furnivall sequence, with lacunae and linking passages supplied from named manuscripts, the prose Retraction, and a separately identified spurious Gamelyn appendix. The twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are the numbered intellectual extent; Gamelyn, a rejected Clerk link, other appended links, and the woodcut illustration suites are apparatus and do not increase that count. The unit is not the abstract Canterbury Tales tradition, any one homogeneous manuscript transcription, the complete physical Gg.4.27(1) anthology, the printed carrier, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalog_summary.date: Canterbury Tales composed probably from the late 1380s through 1400; principal witness dated 1410–1440; edition published 1868–1879, with an uncertain parts-issue range through 1884
catalog_summary.region: England; Chaucer and the literary language are associated with London, while the principal Gg.4.27(1) witness was produced in East Anglia, probably near Cambridge

titles.value: The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
notes: Capitalization is normalized for display from the edition and U-M bibliographic title.
This short title excludes the separately identified Gamelyn appendix and modern editorial/visual apparatus.

agents.name: Geoffrey Chaucer
notes: The edition and U-M record identify Chaucer as author; the separately labeled Gamelyn appendix is not assigned to him here.

agents.name: Frederick James Furnivall
notes: Authority identifiers, dates 1825–1910, profession, and Chaucer Society association distinguish the editor from namesakes.

agents.name: Anonymous author of Gamelyn
notes: A local responsibility placeholder for the unidentified medieval author; it does not imply collective composition or identify a historical person.

scope.description: The Canterbury Tales, including the frame, twenty-four tales, securely Chaucerian prologues, epilogues, and Retraction; excluding the Gamelyn appendix, Furnivall's rejected Clerk link, spurious links, and any supplied linking wording whose authorship remains unverified

assertion.notes: Furnivall explicitly labels the appendix spurious; no named author is assigned in the checked edition evidence.

scope.description: The principal Gg.4.27 text and explicitly identified passages supplied from other manuscripts
Assembly of the manuscript-specific text, named supplied passages, appendices, and illustration suites

date_statements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame, and exact dates remain conjectural. This is not the date of Gg.4.27(1) or Furnivall's edition.

date_statements.display: Principal Gg.4.27(1) manuscript: 1410–1440; Binski and Zutshi narrow it to approximately 1420–1430

places.label: East Anglia
notes: The CUDL description places production of Gg.4.27(1) somewhere in East Anglia, probably not far east of Cambridge; this is a witness-production claim, not a whole-work composition site.

The two prose tales and the prose Retraction.

summaries.value: Furnivall's one-manuscript Chaucer Society edition presents the unfinished Canterbury Tales chiefly from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27, filling documented losses and links from seven identified manuscripts plus one unresolved witness labeled Z, E, or I. Its ten groups contain the conventional twenty-four tales—twenty-two verse and two prose—within a pilgrimage frame, followed by the prose Retraction. The printed volume also appends the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn, textual links, twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript, and six teller plus six allegorical figures copied from Gg.4.27.

Furnivall orders the ten internally linked groups as A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, C/IV, B-beta/III, G/VIII, H/IX, and I/X. This is the edition's sequence, not a claim that Chaucer fixed a final order.

The XML serializes the title page, ten main groups, Gamelyn appendix, and page milestones across 738 source images, but deliberately labels broad front and back sections omitted. The complete printed scan shows additional textual and visual appendices. Representation counts and the 738 PB sequence therefore describe the encoding, not the number of intellectual sections or a complete transcription of every printed surface.

extent.description: The intellectual extent is the conventional twenty-four Canterbury tales in ten macro-fragments: twenty-two verse tales and two prose tales (Melibee and the Parson's Tale), plus non-tale prologues, links, epilogues, and the prose Retraction. The sections field therefore counts the ten internally linked fragment groups, not every editorial subdivision. Gamelyn and visual/textual appendices are apparatus and are assigned item_count 0. The repository XML contains 10 main BODY DIV1 groups, 51 DIV2 elements, 69 DIV3 elements, 18,286 L elements, 609 LG elements, 233 P elements, 1,209 MILESTONE elements, and 738 uniquely sequenced PB elements. The verse_groups and verse_lines fields remain null because raw LG/L totals include prose serialized as lines, rubrics, supplied passages, and editorial structure and have not been semantically reduced to defensible verse metrics.

content_parts.label: Group A, Fragment I
content_parts.summary: General Prologue and the Knight, Miller, Reeve, and Cook sequence; Furnivall fills General Prologue gaps from Harley 1758 and Sloane 1685 and prints the otherwise lost Cook's Tale from Sloane 1685.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Pilgrimage-frame prologue; Gg begins within the text, and Furnivall marks supplied gaps from Harley 1758 and Sloane 1685.

content_parts.label: The Knight's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale; the Gg witness is extant at ff. 145r–174v, with losses documented by the catalog and edition.

content_parts.label: The Miller's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse fabliau, preceded by its prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 176r–184v.

content_parts.label: The Reeve's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse fabliau, preceded by its prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 186v–192r.

content_parts.label: The Cook's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse fragment; Gg loses the tale after its prologue, so Furnivall supplies the printed text from Sloane MS 1685.

content_parts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
content_parts.summary: Man of Law sequence in Furnivall's edition order.

content_parts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale with introductory and prologue material; the Gg tale occupies ff. 196r–210v.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence; Furnivall supplies the Wife–Friar and Friar–Summoner links from Harley MS 1758.

content_parts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse romance preceded by the substantial Wife of Bath's Prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 222v–227v.

content_parts.label: The Friar's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale; the Gg witness preserves the tale at ff. 229r–232v but lacks its prologue.

content_parts.label: The Summoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale preceded by its prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 235r–243v.

content_parts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence, including an appended rejected Clerk end-link and supplied Merchant material from Sloane 1685, Dd.4.24, and Harley 7335.

content_parts.label: The Clerk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale with prologue and envoy; the Gg text occupies ff. 244r–259v, while Furnivall supplies the head-link and proem from Sloane 1685.

content_parts.label: The Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale; Gg preserves the tale at ff. 261r–275v but lacks its prologue. Furnivall supplies the Merchant's Prologue from Dd.4.24 and the end-link from Harley 7335.

content_parts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
content_parts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence; Furnivall supplies links and Franklin's proem from Harley 7335 and Sloane 1685.

content_parts.label: The Squire's Tale
content_parts.summary: Unfinished verse romance; the Gg text occupies ff. 277r–284v.

content_parts.label: The Franklin's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse romance/breton lay; the Gg text occupies ff. 287r–297v.

content_parts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence; Furnivall supplies the link and first portion of the Pardoner's preamble from Sloane 1685.

content_parts.label: The Physician's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale; Gg preserves only a short surviving portion at ff. 302r–302v because surrounding illustrated leaves were removed.

content_parts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse exemplum with prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 306r–312v.

content_parts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
content_parts.summary: Shipman, Prioress, Sir Thopas, Melibee, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence, with several links supplied from Sloane 1685, Royal 18 C II, and Royal 17 D XV.

content_parts.label: The Shipman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse fabliau; the Gg text occupies ff. 314r–317v.

content_parts.label: The Prioress's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse Marian miracle tale; the Gg text occupies ff. 321r–322v.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: Unfinished verse parody of romance; the Gg text occupies ff. 323r–324v.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Melibee
content_parts.summary: Prose moral tale; the Gg text occupies ff. 327r–349v, and Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions.

content_parts.label: The Monk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse collection of tragedies; the Gg text occupies ff. 352r–362v.

content_parts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse beast fable; the Gg text occupies ff. 362v–371v, while Furnivall appends an end-link from Royal MS 17 D XV.

content_parts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

content_parts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse saint's life; the Gg text occupies ff. 375r–380v.

content_parts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse alchemical satire with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 381r–393v.

content_parts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
content_parts.summary: Manciple sequence.

content_parts.label: The Manciple's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse moral fable with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 393v–398v.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Parson sequence and Chaucer's Retraction; Furnivall supplies the concluding leave-taking/Retraction passage from Harley MS 1758.

content_parts.label: The Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prose penitential treatise with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 398v–443v, and Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions.

content_parts.label: Retraction
content_parts.summary: Prose authorial leave-taking at the end of the Parson sequence; Gg ends on f. 443v, with the edition's final supplied passage identified as Harley 1758.

content_parts.label: The spurious Tale of Gamelyn
content_parts.summary: A complete verse appendix transcribed by Furnivall from Sloane MS 1685 and explicitly labeled spurious; it is not counted among Chaucer's twenty-four tales.

content_parts.label: Teller drawings and allegorical figures
content_parts.summary: Printed woodcuts by W. H. Hooper: twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript, followed by six teller and six Vice/Virtue figures copied from Gg.4.27. The XML's omitted back-matter division retains page milestones but not a textual encoding of this visual suite.

locators.value: “Drawings of the 23 Tellers” and “Drawings of 6 Tellers ... and 6 Allegorical Figures”

evidence.citation: Pinned CME source XML for agz8234.
evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item AGZ8234.0001.001, the title, Chaucer and Furnivall, the 1868–1879 edition, source extent, series numbers, 2006 U-M publication layer, OCR method, and omitted front/back treatment.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual inspection of the pinned agz8234 XML.
evidence.locator: TEXT/FRONT, TEXT/BODY/DIV1[1–10], TEXT/BACK/DIV1; counts of DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, HEAD, L, LG, MILESTONE, NOTE1, P, and PB
evidence.summary: The source has ten main groups in Furnivall order, a Gamelyn appendix, broad omitted front/back divisions, 13 DIV1, 51 DIV2, 69 DIV3, 203 HEAD, 18,286 L, 609 LG, 1,209 MILESTONE, 1 NOTE, 233 P, and 738 PB elements. PB sequence and references are unique and continuous from 1 through 738.
notes: Counts are representation metrics. They are not canonical numbers of tales, lines, stanzas, or manuscript leaves.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, AGZ8234.0001.001 item information and table of contents.
evidence.locator: Title, author, publication, rights, and contents
evidence.summary: Confirms the bibliographic title, Chaucer attribution, 1868–1879 publication, public digital text, rights assertion, ten group order, Retraction, Gamelyn appendix, and omitted divisions.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales (London: Chaucer Society, 1868–1879), complete Oxford/Bodleian scan.
evidence.locator: title page; contents; source notices throughout the main text; Gamelyn and link appendices; teller and allegorical drawing appendices
evidence.summary: Direct inspection confirms Furnivall's responsibility, edition sequence, named supplied passages, spurious Gamelyn appendix, retained Tyrwhitt paragraphing, and the two visual source suites copied from Ellesmere and Gg.4.27.
notes: The cached Bodleian PDF has 785 image pages and preserves the complete carrier. Internet Archive metadata reports a different parts-issue range and contains an apparent ‘Sloane 1865’ typo for the Gamelyn source; the edition itself says Sloane 1685.

evidence.citation: Cambridge Digital Library, Anthology of Geoffrey Chaucer's literary works, with other texts, Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(1).
evidence.locator: MS description; item 7 and child items 8–44; decoration description; IIIF canvases 275–902
evidence.summary: The institutional catalog dates the main manuscript 1410–1440, localizes it to England/East Anglia, describes losses and later replacement leaves, places the Canterbury Tales at ff. 132r–443v, enumerates its component loci, and locates the six surviving teller images and three Vice/Virtue pairs.

evidence.citation: Helen Cooper, The Canterbury Tales, 3rd ed., Oxford Guides to Chaucer, chapter 1, “The Canterbury Tales” (Oxford University Press, 2023).
evidence.summary: Supports the cautious late-1380s-through-1400 composition range, the work's unfinished condition, the possibility that individual tales predate the frame, and major genre/subject classifications.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “Fragments or Groups of Tales.”
evidence.summary: Explains the ten internally linked fragments, the absence of a securely authorial final order, and the work's unfinished state.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, “The General Prologue.”
evidence.summary: Supports the pilgrimage frame from Southwark toward Canterbury and the General Prologue's social-estates portraiture.

evidence.citation: Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek/GND authority record for Frederick James Furnivall.
evidence.summary: Identifies Frederick James Furnivall (1825–1910) as a philologist, literary historian, and editor and supplies authority identifiers compatible with the edition's named editor.

Provides stable entity IDs and passage-scoped relationships for the repository artifact, U-M encoding, Furnivall edition, principal Gg witness, supplied manuscripts, source facsimiles, and access/rights layers.

open_questions.question: Were the eight Chaucer Society parts all issued by 1879, or did some parts or appended plates appear through 1884?
next_steps: Consult Chaucer Society annual lists, wrappers, and a bibliographic census of separately issued parts.
Retain the title-page range 1868–1879 and the U-M/Internet Archive ‘1868–84?’ note as distinct attributed statements until reconciled.

open_questions.question: What composition or revision dates can be supported for each individual tale and link, as distinct from the broad late-1380s-through-1400 range and manuscript attestation dates?
next_steps: Consult tale-level current scholarship before adding narrower date statements.
Keep witness production dates and Furnivall publication dates separate from medieval composition.

open_questions.question: Should the non-tale prologues, links, epilogues, and rejected Clerk link receive their own metadata parts rather than remaining summarized within the ten fragments and tale records?
next_steps: Add component records only after every edition unit is aligned to stable work identifiers and exact source scopes.
Until then, retain content_structure_status=summary and do not equate the XML's 51 DIV2 elements with 51 intellectual works.

notes: The work record deliberately preserves Furnivall's Gg-specific composite representation while keeping every physical, editorial, and digital layer in the lineage manifest.
Gg.4.27(2), a separately bound fourteen-leaf quire once placed after part (1), does not contain this Canterbury Tales text and is not treated as a missing portion of the target witness.
The printed word ‘spurious’ and current attribution practice exclude Gamelyn from the Chaucerian twenty-four-tale extent without erasing its presence in the edition and XML.
Genres and subjects describe the represented texts, not every work in the complete Gg.4.27(1) anthology or every source named in Furnivall's notes.
